_meta.note: Evidence-backed food and lifestyle interventions per species deficit. Each intervention is grounded in at least one peer-reviewed study. Avoid making drug-level efficacy claims.

increase_with.name: Pomegranate extract or juice
increase_with.mechanism: Pomegranate polyphenols (ellagitannins) are converted by gut bacteria to urolithins, which selectively promote Akkermansia growth and strengthen the mucus layer
increase_with.evidence: Multiple RCTs and observational studies; Anhê FF et al. (2015) Gut; Henning SM et al. (2019) Eur J Nutr

increase_with.name: Green tea (EGCG)
increase_with.mechanism: Epigallocatechin gallate (EGCG) promotes Akkermansia growth directly and protects mucus layer integrity
increase_with.evidence: Henning SM et al. (2019); multiple animal studies showing consistent Akkermansia enrichment

increase_with.name: Cranberry polyphenols
increase_with.dose: 240mL unsweetened cranberry juice or 500mg cranberry extract daily
increase_with.mechanism: A-type proanthocyanidins from cranberry specifically promote Akkermansia proliferation in the mucus layer
increase_with.evidence: Anhê FF et al. (2015) Gut — cranberry polyphenols reversed high-fat diet-induced Akkermansia decline

increase_with.name: Reduce ultra-processed foods
increase_with.dose: Replace packaged snacks with whole-food alternatives
increase_with.mechanism: Emulsifiers (carboxymethylcellulose, polysorbate-80) in ultra-processed foods degrade the mucus layer that Akkermansia colonises
increase_with.evidence: Chassaing B et al. (2015) Nature — dietary emulsifiers erode mucus layer and deplete Akkermansia

avoid: High-fat, low-fibre diet patterns — consistently deplete Akkermansia
Some artificial sweeteners (saccharin, sucralose) — shown to reduce Akkermansia in mouse models

increase_with.name: Jerusalem artichoke (sunchoke)
increase_with.mechanism: Highest natural source of inulin-type fructans (FOS). F. prausnitzii preferentially ferments FOS, producing butyrate. Start small — rapid FOS increase causes gas initially.
increase_with.evidence: Multiple prebiotic trials show FOS selectively increases Lachnospiraceae and Ruminococcaceae including F. prausnitzii

increase_with.name: Chicory root / inulin supplement
increase_with.dose: 5g/day starting dose, increase to 10g over 2 weeks
increase_with.mechanism: Inulin is the direct prebiotic substrate for F. prausnitzii fermentation. Go slow — bloating is common in week 1 as your gut adapts.
increase_with.evidence: Holscher HD et al. (2015) Journal of Nutrition — inulin increased Faecalibacterium abundance in healthy adults

increase_with.name: Oats (beta-glucan)
increase_with.mechanism: Beta-glucan from oats is fermented by Lachnospiraceae and Ruminococcaceae, producing butyrate and supporting F. prausnitzii indirectly
increase_with.evidence: Demonstrated in multiple fibre intervention RCTs

increase_with.name: Leeks, onions, garlic (cooked)
increase_with.mechanism: Natural FOS and fructooligosaccharide sources. Cooking reduces the gas-producing effect while preserving prebiotic activity.
increase_with.evidence: Dietary patterns high in allium vegetables consistently associate with higher butyrate-producing bacteria

increase_with.name: Cooled cooked potato / rice (resistant starch)
increase_with.dose: 1 serving cooled, then reheated or eaten cold (potato salad, cold rice)
increase_with.mechanism: Cooling converts digestible starch to resistant starch type 3 (RS3). RS3 is fermented by Lachnospiraceae, increasing butyrate production and supporting F. prausnitzii.
increase_with.evidence: Baxter NT et al. (2019) Cell Host Microbe — RS3 intervention increased Lachnospiraceae and butyrate production

avoid: Low-fibre, high-animal-protein diets — starve F. prausnitzii
Antibiotics — dramatically reduce F. prausnitzii; recovery can take months
High sugar intake — feeds E. coli and Proteobacteria preferentially

reduce_with.name: Fermented foods (kimchi, kefir, live-culture yoghurt)
reduce_with.mechanism: Lactobacillus and Bifidobacterium from fermented foods compete with E. coli for gut niche, reducing bloom. Also lower gut pH, which E. coli dislikes.
reduce_with.evidence: Wastyk HC et al. (2021) Cell — high-fermented-food diet increased microbiome diversity and reduced inflammatory markers

reduce_with.name: Reduce refined sugar and white flour
reduce_with.mechanism: Simple sugars are preferentially fermented by Proteobacteria including E. coli. Removing this substrate reduces competitive advantage.
reduce_with.evidence: Dietary patterns high in refined carbohydrates consistently associate with Proteobacteria expansion

increase_with.name: Fermented dairy (yoghurt with live cultures, kefir)
increase_with.mechanism: Dairy Bifidobacterium strains transiently colonise the gut and create conditions (lower pH, acetate production) that support resident Bifidobacterium longum
increase_with.evidence: Standard dietary guideline; multiple RCTs confirm transient Bifidobacterium enrichment

increase_with.name: Galacto-oligosaccharides (GOS) — from legumes
increase_with.dose: Lentils, chickpeas, black beans 3–4 times per week
increase_with.mechanism: GOS is the primary prebiotic substrate for Bifidobacterium. Legumes are the best natural source.
increase_with.evidence: Vulevic J et al. (2015) Gut — GOS significantly increased Bifidobacterium in a double-blind RCT
